policy_statement: Develop community-driven programs/tools (e.g., labeling, apps, education, incentives) to equitably make pro-climate, pro-health food choices affordable and culturally relevant.
mechanism_description: Community-designed labeling, education, apps, and incentives shift diets toward lower-carbon foods, reducing emissions associated with food production and consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Diet choice / food demand shift → ↓ high-emissions food production (enteric CH₄, manure N₂O/CH₄, feed/land-use pressures, energy use) → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Household and institutional food purchasing/consumption patterns are influenced via labeling, education, apps, nudges, and financial/availability incentives to favor lower-carbon foods.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive products decreases associated agricultural production and supply-chain activity, cutting enteric methane from ruminants, manure-related methane and nitrous oxide, fertilizer-driven N₂O, and energy-related CO₂ in processing/transport; it can also reduce land demand pressure that otherwise drives land-use change.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in food-system lifecycle GHG emissions (CO₂e), primarily methane and nitrous oxide reductions plus some CO₂ reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering lifecycle food-system GHG emissions by shifting consumption from high-emissions foods to lower-emissions options.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Behavior/information/incentives → diet shift → lower-emissions production/supply chain → ↓ GHGs (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While programs may also improve health, affordability, or reduce waste, the mechanism as defined targets emissions reductions via demand-side food choices; that is a direct mitigation pathway rather than adaptation, resource efficiency (same service with less input), or nature-based solutions (ecosystem enhancement).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program primarily reduces food waste, increases local sourcing, or boosts regenerative agriculture, those would be different mechanisms/categories; here the defining lever is diet composition toward lower-carbon foods.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that uses labeling/education/apps/incentives to shift diets toward lower-carbon foods shares the same dominant effect—reduced lifecycle food-related GHG emissions—so Mitigation remains the primary category across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy emphasizes equity, health co-benefits, procurement vs. consumer tools, or specific food categories is policy-specific evidence and should be handled at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implementation relies mainly on “buy local” claims without changing diet composition, emissions impacts can be ambiguous; similarly, promoting certain low-carbon foods that are resource-intensive (e.g., heated greenhouses, air-freighted perishables) could weaken benefits—these are policy-design edge cases but do not change the primary mechanism classification.
key_indicators: “Incentivize pro-climate, prohealth food choices”; “Develop… programs and tools”; “labeling, apps, education, incentives”; “make affordable and culturally relevant… food choices”
co_benefits: Public health and nutrition improvements; equity/affordability; cultural relevance and community empowerment
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes mixed instrument elements (information tools like labeling/education/apps plus incentives). Classified as an incentive-program because “Incentivize” and affordability support are the dominant levers; no explicit regulatory/procurement mandate is stated.
